title: Static Analysis & Fuzz Testing
description: Master automated security testing: static code analysis tools, dynamic fuzz testing, and how to integrate them into your development workflow
learning_outcomes: Explain what static analysis is and how it differs from dynamic analysis
Describe how static analysis tools parse code and build intermediate representations
Identify what static analysers can find: null pointers, buffer overflows, SQL injection, resource leaks, race conditions
Differentiate true positives, false positives, and false negatives in tool output
Use taint analysis to trace untrusted data from sources to sinks
Compare open-source static analysers: SonarQube, SpotBugs, PMD, CodeQL, Checkmarx
Explain what fuzz testing is and why it complements traditional testing
Differentiate black-box, grey-box, and white-box fuzzing
Describe how AFL uses coverage-guided mutation to discover crashes
Set up and run AFL with seed inputs, interpret crash and hang outputs
Integrate static analysis and fuzz testing into a CI/CD pipeline

explanation_styles.analogy: Static analysis is like a SPELL CHECKER for your code. It reads every line without running the program, looking for patterns that usually mean bugs — like a spell checker flags 'teh' as probably being 'the'. Fuzz testing is like a CRASH TEST — you throw your program against random inputs to see what breaks, just like car manufacturers crash cars into walls to find weaknesses.
explanation_styles.visual: SOURCE CODE --> [Static Analyser] --> Warning List (true positives + false positives). RUNNING PROGRAM --> [Fuzzer sends random data] --> Crash? Hang? New path? --> Save interesting inputs --> Mutate and repeat.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Static Analysis: Step 1: Tool parses source code. Step 2: Builds AST (Abstract Syntax Tree). Step 3: Checks rules (e.g., 'never concatenate user input into SQL'). Step 4: Reports warnings with severity. Step 5: Developer triages: true positive or false positive?
explanation_styles.code_trace: Taint trace: request.getParameter('name') [TAINTED] --> String query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE name=' + name [TAINTED reaches SQL sink] --> ALERT: SQL Injection!
explanation_styles.comparison: SAST: Analyses code, finds bugs early, many false positives, fast. DAST: Analyses running app, fewer false positives, finds runtime issues, needs deployed app. Fuzzing: Random input testing, finds crashes, great for unknown bugs, takes time.

theory.introduction:
Imagine you are building a bridge. Before anyone drives across it, you would want two types of inspections.

First, a BLUEPRINT REVIEW: an engineer examines the design drawings looking for structural weaknesses — beams that are too thin, connections that might fail under stress, materials that do not meet the specification. This is STATIC ANALYSIS. You examine the plan (source code) without actually building anything (running the program).

Second, a STRESS TEST: you put actual weight on the bridge, shake it with machines, expose it to wind and water, and see what breaks. This is FUZZ TESTING (and dynamic analysis). You run the actual program and throw unexpected inputs at it to find crashes, hangs, and security holes.

Neither inspection alone is enough. The blueprint review might miss problems that only appear under real stress. The stress test might not cover every part of the bridge. Together, they catch far more problems than either alone.

Static Analysis Tools (also called SAST — Static Application Security Testing) have been used since 1979 when Steve Johnson created Lint for C. Modern tools like SonarQube, SpotBugs, CodeQL, and Checkmarx can find SQL injection, XSS, path traversal, null pointer dereferences, buffer overflows, race conditions, and resource leaks — all without running your code.

Fuzz Testing was pioneered in 1988 by Barton Miller at the University of Wisconsin. His students sent random data to Unix utilities and found that 25-33% of them crashed! Modern fuzzers like AFL (American Fuzzy Lop), libFuzzer, and HoneyFuzz use coverage-guided mutation to intelligently explore program paths and find bugs that humans would never think to test for. Google's OSS-Fuzz has found over 30,000 bugs in open-source software using these techniques.
theory.why_important: Manual code review cannot scale — a large application has millions of lines of code. Static analysis automates the review process, finding common vulnerability patterns in seconds. Fuzz testing finds bugs that even careful developers miss, because it tests inputs that no human would think to try. Together, they form the backbone of modern DevSecOps. Every major tech company (Google, Microsoft, Apple, Meta) uses both static analysis and fuzz testing in their development pipelines. The Heartbleed bug in OpenSSL (2014), which affected millions of websites, was later found to be detectable by both static analysis and fuzzing. These tools are not optional — they are essential for secure software development.

key_concepts.explanation:
Static Application Security Testing (SAST) analyses source code, bytecode, or binaries WITHOUT executing the program. The tool parses the code like a compiler does, builds an intermediate representation (like an Abstract Syntax Tree), and then applies rules to detect patterns that indicate vulnerabilities.

What SAST can find:
- SQL injection (string concatenation in queries)
- Cross-site scripting (unescaped output)
- Path traversal (user input in file paths)
- Null pointer dereferences
- Buffer overflows
- Resource leaks (unclosed streams)
- Race conditions
- Hard-coded credentials
- Weak cryptography
- Type mismatches

SAST works early in development — you can scan code before it even compiles successfully. This is called 'shifting security left' because you find problems earlier in the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) when they are cheapest to fix.
key_concepts.ai_hint: SAST = spell-checker for security. Reads code, finds patterns that usually mean bugs. Works without running the program.

key_concepts.explanation:
Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) analyses the application while it is RUNNING. The tool sends requests to the application (like a real user or attacker would) and examines the responses for signs of vulnerability.

DAST pros: fewer false positives (it tests real behaviour), finds runtime issues, tests the deployed configuration.
DAST cons: needs a running application, slower, cannot find all code paths, only finds issues in the code that executes.

Common DAST tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Nikto, Acunetix.

DAST complements SAST — use BOTH for maximum coverage.
key_concepts.ai_hint: DAST = attacking your running app from outside. Sends real requests, checks real responses. Like a robot penetration tester.

key_concepts.concept: How Static Analysers Work: Parsing, AST, Rules
key_concepts.explanation:
Static analysers work like compilers with extra steps:

1. LEXING: Convert source code into tokens (keywords, identifiers, operators)
2. PARSING: Build an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) — a tree structure representing the code
3. SEMANTIC ANALYSIS: Understand types, scope, data flow
4. RULE CHECKING: Apply security rules to the AST

The tool does NOT know what your program is SUPPOSED to do. It looks for violations of good programming practices and specific error patterns. Think of it as a very thorough proofreader who knows the grammar rules but has not read the book's plot.

Modern tools go beyond pattern matching. They use:
- Data flow analysis: tracking where data moves through the program
- Control flow analysis: understanding which code paths are possible
- Taint analysis: tracking untrusted input from source to sink
- Inter-procedural analysis: following data across method calls
key_concepts.ai_hint: The tool builds a tree of your code, then traces data flow from untrusted sources (user input) to dangerous sinks (SQL, file system, HTML output).

key_concepts.explanation:
Taint analysis is the most powerful technique in static analysis. It tracks data that comes from untrusted SOURCES (user input, files, network, database) through the program to dangerous SINKS (SQL queries, HTML output, file operations, command execution).

SOURCES (where tainted data enters):
- request.getParameter()
- request.getInputStream()
- System.getenv()
- File reading
- Database results

SINKS (where tainted data is dangerous):
- SQL: Statement.executeQuery()
- XSS: response.getWriter().write()
- Command: Runtime.exec()
- File: new File()
- Deserialisation: ObjectInputStream.readObject()

SANITISERS (functions that clean tainted data):
- PreparedStatement (for SQL)
- HtmlUtils.htmlEscape() (for XSS)
- Regex whitelist validation

If tainted data reaches a sink WITHOUT passing through a sanitiser, the tool reports a vulnerability.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Source -> Propagation -> Sink. If no sanitiser in between = vulnerability. PreparedStatement is a sanitiser for SQL.

key_concepts.concept: True Positives, False Positives, False Negatives
key_concepts.explanation:
Static analysis tools are not perfect. Their output contains:

TRUE POSITIVE (TP): A real vulnerability that the tool correctly identified. This needs to be fixed.

FALSE POSITIVE (FP): The tool flagged something as a vulnerability, but it is actually safe. This wastes developer time investigating non-issues.

FALSE NEGATIVE (FN): A real vulnerability that the tool MISSED. This is the most dangerous because you think you are safe but are not.

TRUE NEGATIVE (TN): Safe code that the tool correctly did not flag.

The challenge with static analysis is balancing false positives and false negatives. A tool that flags EVERYTHING has zero false negatives but massive false positives (noisy). A tool that only flags obvious issues has few false positives but may miss subtle bugs (dangerous).

Industry average: 30-60% false positive rate for SAST tools. Developers must TRIAGE the warnings — examine each one and decide if it is real.
key_concepts.ai_hint: True positive = real bug found. False positive = false alarm. False negative = missed bug (the dangerous one!). Developers must triage every warning.

key_concepts.concept: Static Analysis Tools: SonarQube, SpotBugs, PMD, CodeQL
key_concepts.explanation:
Key open-source static analysis tools:

SONARQUBE: The most popular. Supports 30+ languages. Web-based dashboard. Tracks code quality over time. Categories: Bugs, Vulnerabilities, Code Smells, Security Hotspots. Free Community Edition available.

SPOTBUGS (successor to FindBugs): Java-specific. Analyses bytecode (.class files). Fast and accurate for Java. Finds null pointers, resource leaks, synchronisation issues, security bugs.

PMD: Multi-language (Java, JS, XML, etc.). Rule-based. Finds dead code, empty catch blocks, unnecessary object creation, copy-paste code. Good for code quality.

CODEQL: Created by GitHub/Semmle. Treats code as DATA you can query with a SQL-like language. Very powerful for finding complex vulnerability patterns. Free for open-source projects.

CHECKMARX: Commercial enterprise SAST. Excellent taint analysis. Supports many languages. Expensive but comprehensive.

FORTIFY: HP/Micro Focus. Enterprise-grade. Strong Java support.
key_concepts.ai_hint: SonarQube = most popular, web dashboard. SpotBugs = Java-specific. CodeQL = query code like a database. All free for open source.

key_concepts.explanation:
Fuzz testing (fuzzing) is an automated software testing technique that feeds RANDOM, UNEXPECTED, or MALFORMED data to a program to find crashes, hangs, memory errors, and security vulnerabilities.

The concept is simple: programs are designed to handle valid input. What happens when you send INVALID input? Many programs crash, leak memory, or behave unexpectedly — and these failures often indicate security vulnerabilities.

History: Barton Miller, University of Wisconsin, 1988. His students found that 25-33% of standard Unix utilities crashed when fed random input. In 2020, a follow-up study showed the crash rate had improved but was still 5-12%.

Fuzzing found major bugs in: OpenSSL (Heartbleed), SQLite, PHP, Firefox, Chrome, Linux kernel, and thousands more. Google's OSS-Fuzz runs continuous fuzzing on 1000+ open-source projects and has found 30,000+ bugs.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Fuzz testing = throw random data at your program and see what breaks. It finds bugs humans would never think to test for.

key_concepts.concept: Fuzzing Variations: Mutation vs Generation, Black/Grey/White Box
key_concepts.explanation:
Fuzzing varies along two dimensions:

INPUT CREATION:
- MUTATION-based: Start with valid inputs (seeds), randomly modify them. Change bytes, flip bits, insert data, delete chunks. Simple but effective.
- GENERATION-based: Create inputs from scratch using a grammar or format specification. More targeted but requires knowledge of the input format.

PROGRAM KNOWLEDGE:
- BLACK BOX: No knowledge of program internals. Just send input, observe output. Simple but inefficient — may test the same paths repeatedly.
- GREY BOX: Uses code coverage information to guide testing. If a mutated input reaches NEW code, it is saved as a new seed. This is the most popular approach (AFL, libFuzzer).
- WHITE BOX: Full access to source code and symbolic execution. Most thorough but slowest and hardest to set up.

Most effective combination: Mutation-based + Grey-box (coverage-guided). This is what AFL uses.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Mutation = modify existing valid inputs. Generation = create new inputs from grammar. Grey-box = use coverage to guide mutations. AFL combines all three ideas.

key_concepts.explanation:
AFL is the most influential fuzzer ever created. Written by Michal Zalewski at Google (2013), it revolutionised fuzz testing by using coverage-guided mutation.

How AFL works:
1. COMPILE: Compile the target program with afl-clang or afl-gcc. This inserts instrumentation code that tracks which basic blocks execute.
2. SEED: Provide initial seed inputs — valid examples that the program accepts.
3. MUTATE: AFL randomly modifies a seed input (flip bits, insert bytes, use dictionary values).
4. EXECUTE: Run the program with the mutated input.
5. EVALUATE: Did the mutated input cause a new code path to execute? If YES, save it as a new seed.
6. REPEAT: Continue mutating, executing, evaluating. Focus on seeds that found new paths.

Outputs:
- CRASHES: Inputs that caused the program to crash (segfault, abort). These often indicate memory corruption, buffer overflows, or assertion failures.
- HANGS: Inputs that caused the program to take too long (infinite loops, algorithmic complexity attacks).
- QUEUE: Interesting inputs that discovered new code paths.

AFL has found thousands of vulnerabilities in real software including OpenSSL, PHP, SQLite, Firefox, and the Linux kernel.
key_concepts.ai_hint: AFL: compile with instrumentation, provide seeds, let it mutate and explore. Crashes = potential vulnerabilities. Coverage guidance makes it smart.

key_concepts.explanation:
The SEED CORPUS is the starting point for fuzzing. Seeds should be:
- VALID inputs that the program accepts normally
- SMALL (smaller inputs = faster execution = more tests per second)
- DIVERSE (cover different features and code paths)

MUTATION STRATEGIES that fuzzers use:
- Bit flipping: flip individual bits in the input
- Byte flipping: change individual bytes
- Arithmetic: add/subtract small values from integers
- Interesting values: insert boundary values (0, -1, MAX_INT, powers of 2)
- Block operations: insert, delete, or overwrite chunks of data
- Dictionary: insert known keywords (SQL keywords, HTML tags)
- Splicing: combine parts of two different seeds

COVERAGE FEEDBACK: After each test, the instrumented program reports which basic blocks executed. If a mutated input reached NEW blocks that no previous input reached, it is 'interesting' and saved as a new seed. This creates a feedback loop that drives the fuzzer towards unexplored code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Seeds = starting inputs. Mutations = random changes. Coverage = did we reach new code? If yes, save as new seed. This feedback loop finds deep bugs.

key_concepts.concept: SAST vs DAST vs Fuzzing: When to Use Each
key_concepts.explanation:
Each testing technique has strengths and weaknesses:

SAST (Static Analysis):
- When: During development, in CI/CD pipeline, before code review
- Finds: Code-level bugs, vulnerability patterns, code smells
- Pros: Early detection, no running app needed, covers all code
- Cons: High false positives, cannot find runtime/config issues

DAST (Dynamic Analysis):
- When: After deployment, staging environment
- Finds: Runtime vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, auth issues
- Pros: Few false positives, tests real behaviour
- Cons: Needs running app, slow, limited coverage

FUZZING:
- When: Testing parsers, file handlers, network protocols, APIs
- Finds: Crashes, memory corruption, unexpected behaviour
- Pros: Finds unknown bugs, minimal setup, scales well
- Cons: Takes time, may not find logic bugs, needs crash handling

Best practice: Use ALL THREE in combination. SAST in development, DAST in staging, fuzzing continuously.
key_concepts.ai_hint: SAST = check code. DAST = check running app. Fuzzing = throw random data. Use ALL THREE for maximum security.

key_concepts.explanation:
Modern development uses Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines. Security testing should be automated within these pipelines — not done manually at the end.

A DevSecOps pipeline:
1. Developer pushes code to Git
2. CI server (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) triggers automatically
3. Build the code
4. Run unit tests
5. Run SAST scan (SonarQube, SpotBugs)
6. If SAST finds BLOCKER/CRITICAL issues -> fail the build
7. Deploy to staging
8. Run DAST scan (OWASP ZAP)
9. Run integration tests
10. If all pass -> deploy to production
11. Continuous fuzzing runs in background

The key principle: SHIFT LEFT. Find and fix security issues as early as possible in the development process. A bug found in development costs $100 to fix. The same bug found in production costs $10,000+.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Shift LEFT = find bugs early when they are cheap to fix. Automate security in CI/CD so every code change is checked automatically.

key_concepts.concept: Handling Tool Output: Triage and Prioritisation
key_concepts.explanation:
When a static analyser produces hundreds of warnings, you need a systematic triage process:

1. FILTER by severity: Focus on CRITICAL and HIGH first
2. CATEGORISE: Group by vulnerability type (all SQL injection together)
3. VERIFY: For each warning, determine if it is a true positive or false positive
4. PRIORITISE: Fix true positives by risk (exploitability, impact)
5. SUPPRESS: Mark false positives with annotations so they do not reappear
6. TRACK: Log all decisions for audit purposes

Prioritisation factors:
- CVSS score of the vulnerability type
- Is the vulnerable code reachable from user input?
- Is the application internet-facing?
- What data does the application handle (PII, financial, health)?

SonarQube uses: BLOCKER > CRITICAL > MAJOR > MINOR > INFO
Severity mapping: BLOCKER = must fix before release, CRITICAL = fix soon, MAJOR = fix when possible
key_concepts.ai_hint: Triage: filter by severity, verify each warning, fix true positives, suppress false positives, track decisions. Start with BLOCKER/CRITICAL.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: SonarQube SQL Injection Detection
complete_examples.description: How SonarQube detects and reports SQL injection in Java code.
complete_examples.output:
SonarQube: BLOCKER - SQL Injection detected
Source: username parameter -> Sink: executeQuery()
Fix: Use PreparedStatement with ? placeholders
complete_examples.ai_explanation: SonarQube traces the data flow from the method parameter (source) through string concatenation to executeQuery (sink). Since no sanitiser (PreparedStatement) is found in between, it reports SQL injection. The BLOCKER severity means this must be fixed before release.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if username came from a configuration file instead of a user? Would SonarQube still flag it? (Answer: depends on whether config files are considered untrusted sources in the tool configuration)

complete_examples.title: Example 2: SpotBugs Null Pointer Detection
complete_examples.description: SpotBugs finding a potential null pointer dereference.
complete_examples.output:
SpotBugs: NP_NULL_ON_SOME_PATH - Possible null dereference
findById() may return null, getEmail() called without check
Fix: Add null check or use Optional
complete_examples.ai_explanation: SpotBugs analyses the bytecode and determines that findById() could return null based on its return type and the database pattern. If user is null, calling getEmail() throws NullPointerException, which could crash the application or reveal error information.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: In a security context, how could a null pointer lead to a vulnerability? (Hint: crash = denial of service, error message = information disclosure)

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Resource Leak Detection
complete_examples.description: Static analysis finding an unclosed FileInputStream.
complete_examples.output:
SAST Alert: Resource leak - FileInputStream not closed
Impact: File handle exhaustion -> Denial of Service
Fix: try-with-resources closes stream automatically
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Opening files without closing them leaks OS file handles. Each process has a limited number of file handles (typically 1024 on Linux). If the application leaks handles, eventually new files cannot be opened, causing denial of service. try-with-resources guarantees cleanup.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What other resources can be leaked? (Answer: database connections, network sockets, threads, locks)

complete_examples.title: Example 4: False Positive Identification
complete_examples.description: Learning to identify when a tool is wrong.
complete_examples.output:
Tool says: SQL Injection (BLOCKER)
Reality: FALSE POSITIVE - integer concatenation cannot inject SQL
But: missing error handling IS a real issue (not what the tool flagged)
Best fix: PreparedStatement eliminates both the real and false positive
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is a classic false positive. parseInt() converts the string to an integer, and you cannot inject SQL through integer concatenation. However, a skilled developer would notice the missing error handling and fix both issues. Using PreparedStatement makes the code safe AND silences the false positive.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Why is it still better to use PreparedStatement even though integer concatenation is safe? (Answer: defence in depth, consistency, prevents future bugs if someone changes the type)

complete_examples.title: Example 5: CodeQL Query for Finding SQL Injection
complete_examples.description: Writing a CodeQL query to find SQL injection patterns.
complete_examples.output:
CodeQL finds all paths from HTTP input to SQL execution
Results show source file/line, sink file/line, and data flow path
Developers can see exactly how tainted data reaches the SQL query
complete_examples.ai_explanation: CodeQL is unique because it lets you write custom queries. Instead of relying on predefined rules, you define what sources and sinks matter to YOUR application. The taint tracking engine then finds all possible data flow paths between them.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: How would you modify this query to find XSS instead of SQL injection? (Hint: change the sink from QueryInjectionSink to XssSink)

complete_examples.title: Example 6: AFL Fuzzing a JSON Parser
complete_examples.description: Setting up AFL to fuzz a simple JSON parser and finding a crash.
complete_examples.output:
AFL finds crash with key > 64 characters -> buffer overflow
Crash saved in findings/crashes/
Fix: check length before strncpy, or use dynamic allocation
complete_examples.ai_explanation: AFL mutates the valid JSON seed, trying longer strings, special characters, and boundary values. When it creates a JSON key longer than 64 characters, the strncpy copies too much data into the fixed-size buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow and crash. AFL saves this crashing input for developers to investigate.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: How would you fix this code? (Answer: check end-start < sizeof(key) before copying, or use malloc(end-start+1))

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Mutation Strategies Visualised
complete_examples.description: Showing how AFL mutates inputs step by step.
complete_examples.output:
6 mutation types: bit flip, interesting value, insert, delete, arithmetic, dictionary
Each tests different edge cases automatically
Coverage feedback keeps mutations that reach new code paths
complete_examples.ai_explanation: AFL applies many mutation strategies to each seed input. Bit flips test character handling, interesting values test boundary conditions, insertions test buffer sizes, deletions test incomplete input handling, arithmetic tests numeric overflows, and dictionary mutations test security-relevant strings.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Which mutation strategy is most likely to find SQL injection? (Answer: dictionary mutation with SQL keywords like ' OR 1=1--)

complete_examples.title: Example 8: OWASP ZAP DAST Scan
complete_examples.description: Running a DAST scan with OWASP ZAP against a web application.
complete_examples.output:
ZAP found: 2 HIGH (SQLi, XSS), 2 MEDIUM (headers, cookies), 2 LOW
DAST confirms real vulnerabilities by exploiting them
Complement SAST for full coverage
complete_examples.ai_explanation: OWASP ZAP actively attacks the running application. Unlike SAST which reads code, ZAP sends real attack payloads and checks if the application is vulnerable. This means fewer false positives — if ZAP says it found XSS, it actually injected a script and it executed.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: ZAP found the server is running Apache 2.4.49. Why is this a security issue? (Hint: CVE-2021-41773 — the path traversal vulnerability we learned about!)

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Taint Analysis Deep Dive
complete_examples.description: Complete taint trace through a multi-method Java application.
complete_examples.output:
Taint traced across 3 methods: getParameter -> processInput -> displayResults
trim() and toUpperCase() do NOT remove taint
Tainted data reaches HTML output = XSS
Fix: HtmlUtils.htmlEscape() before output
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Modern SAST tools perform inter-procedural taint analysis — they follow tainted data across method calls and even across classes. trim() and toUpperCase() transform data but do not sanitise it for security purposes. Only security-specific functions like htmlEscape() remove taint.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If processInput() contained: String safe = raw.replaceAll("<", "<"); — would the taint be removed? (Answer: depends on tool configuration, but proper HTML escaping should be recognised as a sanitiser)

complete_examples.title: Example 10: False Negative - What Tools Miss
complete_examples.description: Examples of vulnerabilities that static analysis typically does not catch.
complete_examples.output:
SAST misses: second-order injection, business logic, race conditions
These require manual review, DAST, or specialised tools
No single tool catches everything - use defence in depth
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Static analysis has fundamental limitations. It cannot track data through external systems (databases), it cannot understand business requirements, and it struggles with concurrency issues. This is why you need SAST + DAST + fuzzing + manual code review together.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: How would you find the race condition in example 3? (Answer: concurrency testing tools like ThreadSafe, or stress testing with multiple threads)

complete_examples.title: Example 11: CI/CD Pipeline with Security Gates
complete_examples.description: Complete GitHub Actions pipeline with SAST, DAST, and quality gates.
complete_examples.output:
Pipeline: SAST (SpotBugs+SonarQube) -> DAST (ZAP) -> Fuzz (AFL)
Quality gates fail the build if critical issues found
Shift left: security checked on every code push
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This pipeline automates all three types of security testing. SAST runs first (fast, catches code-level issues). DAST runs second (needs deployed app). Fuzzing runs in parallel (takes longer). If any stage finds critical issues, the pipeline fails and developers must fix before merging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if SonarQube has 30% false positives? (Answer: Use @SuppressWarnings annotations and configure the quality profile to reduce noise)

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Interpreting AFL Output
complete_examples.description: Reading and understanding AFL's status screen.
complete_examples.output:
847 unique paths explored, 3 unique crashes found, 18.4M executions
Still discovering new paths (last: 2 min ago)
3 unique crashes = 3 potential vulnerabilities to investigate
complete_examples.ai_explanation: AFL's status screen is your dashboard. Key metrics: unique crashes (each is a potential vulnerability), total paths (exploration progress), exec speed (performance), last new path (if stale, fuzzing may be exhausted). The 3 unique crashes need manual investigation to determine if they are exploitable.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If 'last new path' was '2 days ago', what would that mean? (Answer: AFL has likely explored all reachable paths and additional fuzzing may not find more. Consider new seeds or different mutation strategies.)

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Java Security Scanning with SpotBugs
complete_examples.description: Running SpotBugs on a Java project and interpreting the security results.
complete_examples.output:
SpotBugs found: 3 HIGH (SQLi, cmd injection, path traversal), 2 MEDIUM, 1 LOW
All are SECURITY category findings
Priority: fix HIGH severity first
complete_examples.ai_explanation: SpotBugs analyses compiled Java bytecode. The -effort:max flag enables the most thorough analysis. Security findings are marked with 'S' category. High priority findings (H) should be fixed immediately. The three HIGH findings represent the most dangerous vulnerability types.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: SpotBugs works on bytecode, not source. What advantage does this have? (Answer: can analyse third-party JARs without source code, catches issues introduced by the compiler)

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Building a Custom Security Rule
complete_examples.description: Creating a custom static analysis rule for your organisation.
complete_examples.output:
Custom rule detects hardcoded credentials by variable name + literal assignment
Flags: apiKey='literal', dbPassword='literal', SECRET='literal'
Passes: values from environment or config files
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Custom rules extend static analysis tools with organisation-specific checks. This rule combines variable name pattern matching with assignment analysis. Any string variable whose name matches sensitive patterns and is assigned a literal string value is flagged. This catches a common mistake that generic tools might miss.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What other custom rules might an organisation need? (Hint: logging PII, using deprecated crypto, accessing production URLs in test code)

visual_diagram.title: Static Analysis & Fuzz Testing: Tools, Workflows & Decision Flow
visual_diagram.content:
STATIC ANALYSIS & FUZZ TESTING
SAST vs DAST vs FUZZING 
  SAST (Static)          DAST (Dynamic)         FUZZING
  Analyses CODE           Analyses RUNNING APP    Throws RANDOM INPUT
  No execution needed     Needs deployed app      Needs compiled program
  Finds: SQLi, XSS,      Finds: XSS, auth,       Finds: crashes, memory
   path traversal,         misconfig, runtime       corruption, hangs,
   null ptrs, leaks        issues, CORS             buffer overflows
  Many false positives    Few false positives     Zero false positives
  All code covered       Only executed code     Coverage-guided
  Fast (minutes)         Medium (hours)         Slow (hours/days)
  Early in SDLC         After deployment      Continuous
TAINT ANALYSIS FLOW 
  SOURCE  request.getParameter()  ── TAINTED ──>
       |
       v
  PROPAGATION  String query = "SELECT..." + input  ── STILL TAINTED ──>
       |
       v
  No sanitiser found...
       |
       v
  SINK  stmt.executeQuery(query)  🚨 ALERT: SQL INJECTION!

  If sanitiser (PreparedStatement) is between source and sink:
  SOURCE -> SANITISER -> SINK = NO ALERT (taint removed) ✅
AFL FUZZING CYCLE 
  1. COMPILE  afl-clang -o target source.c  (add instrumentation)
       |
       v
  2. SEED     Provide valid input files in seeds/ directory
       |
       v
  3. MUTATE   Flip bits, change bytes, insert data, splice
       |
       v
  4. EXECUTE  Run program with mutated input
       |
       v
  5. EVALUATE
       |--- CRASH?  -> Save in crashes/ directory  🚨 BUG FOUND!
       |--- HANG?   -> Save in hangs/ directory
       |--- NEW PATH? -> Save as new seed -> back to MUTATE
       |--- Same paths -> Discard, try next mutation
       |
       v
  REPEAT  Thousands of iterations per second!
TOOL COMPARISON 
  SonarQube    30+ languages, web dashboard, most popular
  SpotBugs     Java bytecode, fast, accurate for Java
  PMD          Multi-language, rules-based, code quality
  CodeQL       Query-based, GitHub, powerful for complex patterns
  Checkmarx    Commercial, enterprise, excellent taint analysis
  AFL          Coverage-guided fuzzer, C/C++ focus
  libFuzzer    In-process fuzzer, LLVM, very fast
  HoneyFuzz    Multi-platform, easy setup
  OWASP ZAP    DAST, web app scanner, free
  Burp Suite   DAST, professional pen testing, commercial
DevSecOps PIPELINE 
  Code Push -> SAST -> Build -> DAST -> Fuzz -> Deploy
  Find bugs EARLY = cheap to fix
  Find bugs LATE = expensive to fix
  SHIFT LEFT = Test security from the start!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: SAST vs DAST
practice_exercises.description: Compare static and dynamic analysis.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SAST: source code, no, many, development, SonarQube, SQLi/XSS patterns
DAST: running app, yes, fewer, staging, OWASP ZAP, runtime issues

hints.message: SAST reads code. DAST attacks the running app.
SAST has more false positives because it cannot confirm exploitability.
SAST: code/no/many/development/SonarQube. DAST: running app/yes/fewer/staging/ZAP.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: What Can Static Analysers Find?
practice_exercises.description: List vulnerability types that SAST detects.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. SQL injection
2. XSS
3. Null pointer dereferences
4. Buffer overflows
5. Resource leaks
6. Race conditions
7. Hard-coded credentials
8. Path traversal

hints.message: Think: injection, memory, resources, concurrency, secrets.
SQL injection, XSS, null pointers, buffer overflows...
All 8: SQLi, XSS, null ptrs, buffer overflow, resource leaks, race conditions, hardcoded creds, path traversal.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Classify Tool Output
practice_exercises.description: Determine if findings are true positive or false positive.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. TRUE POSITIVE (string param directly in SQL)
2. FALSE POSITIVE (integer cannot inject SQL)
3. TRUE POSITIVE (map.get can return null)
4. FALSE POSITIVE (try-with-resources closes it)

hints.message: True positive = the tool is right, there IS a bug.
Integer.parseInt ensures only numbers. try-with-resources auto-closes.
1: TP, 2: FP, 3: TP, 4: FP.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Taint Source Identification
practice_exercises.description: Identify taint sources in Java code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. TAINTED (user input)
2. SAFE (hardcoded literal)
3. TAINTED (environment, attacker-controlled on some systems)
4. SAFE (literal integer)
5. TAINTED (file content could be modified)
6. TAINTED (database content could contain injected data)
7. TAINTED (HTTP header from client)
8. SAFE (generated by system, not user-controlled)

hints.message: Anything from the user, network, file, or database is potentially tainted.
Literals and system-generated values are safe.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Taint Sink Identification
practice_exercises.description: Identify dangerous sinks in Java code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. DANGEROUS (SQL execution)
2. SAFE (console output, not user-facing)
3. DANGEROUS (OS command execution)
4. DANGEROUS (HTML output = XSS)
5. DANGEROUS (file system access = path traversal)
6. SAFE (adding to list)
7. DANGEROUS (deserialization = RCE)
8. DEPENDS (could be log injection if unvalidated)

hints.message: Sinks where tainted data becomes dangerous: SQL, command, HTML, file, deserialization.
Console output and internal data structures are generally safe.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Complete a Taint Trace
practice_exercises.description: Trace tainted data through a code snippet.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.starter_code:
String input = req.getParameter("q");       // Line 1: _____
String cleaned = input.trim();                // Line 2: _____
String upper = cleaned.toUpperCase();         // Line 3: _____
String query = "SELECT * FROM items WHERE name LIKE '%" + upper + "%'"; // Line 4: _____
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(query);      // Line 5: _____

// For each line, is the data TAINTED or CLEAN?
// Is there a vulnerability? If so, what type and CWE?
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Line 1: TAINTED (source)
Line 2: TAINTED (trim doesn't sanitise)
Line 3: TAINTED (toUpperCase doesn't sanitise)
Line 4: TAINTED (concatenated into SQL)
Line 5: TAINTED reaches SQL sink = SQL Injection (CWE-89)

hints.message: trim() and toUpperCase() transform but don't sanitise.
The taint flows all the way from getParameter to executeQuery.
All tainted. SQLi at line 5. Fix: PreparedStatement.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Match Tools to Use Cases
practice_exercises.description: Select the right tool for each scenario.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. SpotBugs (Java bytecode analysis)
2. OWASP ZAP (DAST, free)
3. AFL (fuzz testing C programs)
4. CodeQL (custom queries)
5. SonarQube (multi-language, dashboard)
6. Burp Suite (professional pen testing)

hints.message: SpotBugs = Java bytecode. ZAP = web DAST. AFL = fuzzing.
CodeQL = custom queries. SonarQube = dashboard. Burp = professional pen test.
1:SpotBugs, 2:ZAP, 3:AFL, 4:CodeQL, 5:SonarQube, 6:Burp.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Tool Limitations Quiz
practice_exercises.description: Identify what each tool CANNOT do.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. SAST: runtime misconfigs, auth issues, business logic flaws
2. DAST: dead code vulns, internal logic, full code coverage
3. Fuzzing: logic bugs, auth bypass, design flaws
4. Each finds what others miss — combined coverage is maximum

hints.message: Each tool has blind spots. SAST can't test running configs.
DAST only tests code that executes. Fuzzing finds crashes, not logic.
No single tool finds everything. Defence in depth applies to testing too!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: SonarQube Severity Levels
practice_exercises.description: Map SonarQube severities to actions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
BLOCKER: Fix before release (SQL injection, RCE)
CRITICAL: Fix this sprint (XSS, path traversal)
MAJOR: Fix soon (resource leaks)
MINOR: Backlog (naming conventions)
INFO: Optional (style suggestions)
Decision: CANNOT release — must fix BLOCKER first

hints.message: BLOCKER means the build should fail. Cannot release.
CRITICAL should be fixed quickly but doesn't block release.
Release blocked by BLOCKER. Fix it, then address CRITICALs.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Write Suppression Comments
practice_exercises.description: Suppress false positives with proper documentation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static analysis is your first line of automated defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SpotBugs: @SuppressFBWarnings(value="SQL_INJECTION", justification="userId is parsed integer, cannot inject SQL")
SonarQube: @SuppressWarnings("squid:S3649") // userId is int, not injectable
Always explain WHY it's a false positive so future reviewers understand.

hints.message: Suppression annotations tell the tool to skip this finding.
Always include justification — future developers need to know WHY.
@SuppressWarnings with justification comment. Never suppress without explanation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: What is Fuzz Testing?
practice_exercises.description: Define fuzzing and its purpose.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Automated testing that sends random/malformed input to find bugs
2. Random, mutated, unexpected, boundary-value data
3. Crashes, hangs, memory errors, unexpected behaviour
4. Barton Miller, University of Wisconsin, 1988
5. 25-33% crashed
6. Heartbleed (OpenSSL), bugs in SQLite, PHP, Chrome, Linux kernel

hints.message: Fuzzing = throw random data at programs and see what breaks.
The original study crashed 25-33% of Unix utilities.
Automated random input testing. Found Heartbleed, thousands of bugs in major software.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Mutation vs Generation
practice_exercises.description: Compare the two main input creation strategies.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Mutation: starts with valid seeds, modifies them randomly, easy to set up, may not reach complex code paths
Generation: starts from grammar/spec, creates from scratch, more targeted, requires format knowledge

hints.message: Mutation modifies existing inputs. Generation creates from scratch.
Mutation is simpler. Generation is more targeted.
Mutation: seeds->modify. Generation: grammar->create. Mutation is easier, generation is smarter.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Black, Grey, White Box Fuzzing
practice_exercises.description: Differentiate the three fuzzing approaches.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Black: nothing (input/output only). Simple but inefficient.
Grey: code coverage (which paths executed). Efficient and effective.
White: full source, symbolic execution. Most thorough but slowest.
AFL: Grey box (coverage-guided)

hints.message: Black = blind. Grey = coverage feedback. White = full source analysis.
Grey box is the sweet spot: enough information to be smart, not too slow.
Black: nothing/simple/inefficient. Grey: coverage/efficient/effective. White: everything/thorough/slow. AFL: grey.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Design Seed Inputs
practice_exercises.description: Create effective seed inputs for a fuzzer.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!

hints.message: Seeds should be valid, diverse, and small.
Cover: minimal, typical, edge case, special chars, long strings.
5 diverse seeds covering different code paths.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: AFL Setup Steps
practice_exercises.description: Write the commands to set up and run AFL.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. afl-clang -o target source.c
2. mkdir seeds && echo 'valid input' > seeds/seed1.txt
3. afl-fuzz -i seeds -o findings ./target @@
4. ls findings/crashes/
5. ./target findings/crashes/id:000000,...

hints.message: afl-clang adds instrumentation. -i seeds -o output.
@@ means AFL replaces with the test file path.
Compile, create seeds, run afl-fuzz, check crashes dir, reproduce.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Interpret AFL Status Screen
practice_exercises.description: Read and understand AFL metrics.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. 7 unique crashes = 7 potential bugs
2. Yes, last new path 12 min ago = still active
3. 2100 executions/second = good speed
4. Continue — still finding new paths. Stop when last new path is hours/days ago.

hints.message: 7 unique crashes = 7 different bugs to investigate.
'Last new path: 12 min' means it's still exploring.
7 bugs, still active, good speed, keep running.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Mutation Strategies Exercise
practice_exercises.description: Apply mutations to a seed input.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. GET /api/users/52 HTTP/1.1
2. GET /api/users/-1 HTTP/1.1
3. GET /api/users/42AAAA...100... HTTP/1.1
4. GET /api/u2 HTTP/1.1
5. GET /api/users/' OR 1=1-- HTTP/1.1
6. GET /api/users/2147483689 HTTP/1.1

hints.message: Each strategy targets different code handling: numbers, buffers, parsing, injection.
Dictionary mutation is especially good for finding injection vulnerabilities.
6 mutations, each testing different error handling code.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Fuzzing vs Unit Testing
practice_exercises.description: Compare fuzzing with traditional unit tests.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Unit tests: divide(10,2)=5, divide(0,5)=0, divide(-10,2)=-5, divide(10,-2)=-5, divide(10,0)=exception
Fuzzer extras: MAX_INT/1, MIN_INT/-1 (overflow!), very large values
Both are better — unit tests for known cases, fuzzer for unknown

hints.message: Unit tests check EXPECTED behaviour. Fuzzing checks UNEXPECTED inputs.
MIN_INT / -1 causes integer overflow in many languages!
Both complement each other. Unit tests: known cases. Fuzzing: unknown edge cases.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Coverage-Guided Feedback Loop
practice_exercises.description: Explain how coverage feedback makes fuzzing smarter.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
M1: same paths -> DISCARD
M2: new path -> SAVE AS SEED
M3: same paths as M2 -> DISCARD
M4: new path from -1 seed -> SAVE AS SEED
M2 saved because new coverage. M4 based on -1 because it already reached error handling code, so mutating it is more likely to find deeper errors.

hints.message: Only inputs that reach NEW code are saved.
M2 found the error handler. Mutating M2 can find deeper error handling.
Save only new coverage. Build on interesting seeds to go deeper.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Fuzz Targets for Java
practice_exercises.description: Identify the best targets for fuzzing in a Java application.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fuzz testing finds the bugs nobody thought to test for!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1:5, 2:3, 3:5, 4:1, 5:5, 6:4, 7:1, 8:5
Top 3: JSON parser (complex input parsing), XML parser (XXE + parsing), Image lib (memory corruption in native code). Parsers and file handlers are prime fuzz targets.

hints.message: Best fuzz targets: parsers, file handlers, anything that processes complex input.
Static pages and simple getters have nothing to fuzz.
Parsers (JSON, XML, PDF) and file handlers (image, upload) are prime targets.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Trace XSS Taint
practice_exercises.description: Trace taint flow for a cross-site scripting vulnerability.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
name: TAINTED (source: getParameter)
greeting: TAINTED (concatenation propagates)
Sink: HTML output
Vulnerability: Reflected XSS
CWE: CWE-79
Fix: HtmlUtils.htmlEscape(name) before concatenation

hints.message: getParameter = tainted source.
String concatenation propagates taint.
Tainted -> tainted -> HTML sink = XSS. Fix with htmlEscape.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Trace Command Injection Taint
practice_exercises.description: Trace taint for an OS command injection.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
host: TAINTED
cmd: TAINTED
Sink: OS command execution
Vulnerability: Command Injection (CWE-78)
Attack: host = "; rm -rf /"
Fix: Whitelist validation (IP regex), use ProcessBuilder with args array

hints.message: getParameter is the source. exec() is the command sink.
Attacker sends: 8.8.8.8; rm -rf / to chain commands.
Taint reaches exec() = command injection. Fix: validate input, use ProcessBuilder.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Trace Path Traversal Taint
practice_exercises.description: Trace taint for a path traversal vulnerability.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
file: TAINTED
f: TAINTED path
Sink: file system read
Vulnerability: Path Traversal (CWE-22)
Attack: file=../../../etc/passwd
Fix: whitelist regex + canonical path check

hints.message: User input goes directly into new File().
../../../etc/passwd escapes the uploads directory.
Tainted -> File constructor -> read sink = path traversal. Fix: validate + canonical.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Find the Sanitiser
practice_exercises.description: Identify which code adds a sanitiser that removes taint.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A: NOT sanitised (trim and toLowerCase don't prevent SQL injection)
B: SANITISED (PreparedStatement is a proper SQL sanitiser)
C: PARTIALLY sanitised (whitelist blocks injection chars, but concatenation is still bad practice)

hints.message: trim() and toLowerCase() are transforms, not sanitisers.
PreparedStatement is the definitive SQL sanitiser.
A: no, B: yes (PreparedStatement), C: partial (whitelist helps but use PreparedStatement).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Multi-Method Taint Trace
practice_exercises.description: Trace taint across method boundaries.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
data: TAINTED, processed: TAINTED (toUpperCase doesn't sanitise), render reaches HTML sink: TAINTED
Vulnerability: Stored/Reflected XSS (CWE-79)
Taint flows across 3 classes!

hints.message: Taint flows from Controller to Service to View.
toUpperCase() does not remove taint.
Cross-class taint: getParameter -> process (still tainted) -> render HTML sink = XSS.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Identify All Sinks in a Class
practice_exercises.description: Find every dangerous sink in a code sample.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Line 5: SQL Injection (CWE-89) - name in query
Line 6: Command Injection (CWE-78) - cmd in exec
Line 8: Path Traversal (CWE-22) - file in FileInputStream
Line 9: Reflected XSS (CWE-79) - name in HTML output
4 vulnerabilities, 3 tainted sources, 4 dangerous sinks

hints.message: 3 parameters, 4 sinks. Each parameter reaches at least one sink.
name reaches TWO sinks: SQL and HTML.
4 vulns: SQLi(L5), cmd injection(L6), path traversal(L8), XSS(L9).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Write a Taint Analysis Rule
practice_exercises.description: Define source, sink, and sanitiser for a custom rule.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Sources: request.getParameter(), request.getHeader(), any user input
Sinks: logger.info(), logger.warn(), log.error(), System.out.println() if used for logging
Sanitisers: input.replaceAll("[\\r\\n]", "") or OWASP ESAPI encoder
Vuln: logger.info("Login: " + username)
Attack: username = "admin\nLogin successful for: hacker"

hints.message: Log injection uses \n to create fake log entries.
Sinks are logging methods. Sanitiser removes newlines.
Sources: user input. Sinks: logger methods. Sanitiser: remove \r\n.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Taint Through Database
practice_exercises.description: Trace second-order taint through database storage.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
After storage: data in DB is still attacker-controlled content
retrieved: SHOULD BE TAINTED (came from user, stored in DB)
HTML output: VULNERABLE — Stored XSS!
Most SAST tools lose track of taint when data goes through database.
This is 'second-order' or 'stored' vulnerability.

hints.message: PreparedStatement sanitises for SQL but the DATA is still attacker-controlled.
When retrieved from DB, the data should still be considered tainted.
Second-order: safe storage, dangerous retrieval. SAST tools typically lose taint across DB boundaries.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Complex Taint: Conditional Sanitisation
practice_exercises.description: Analyse taint when sanitisation depends on conditions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output: NOT safe overall. HTML path: safe (htmlEscape). SQL path: NOT safe for HTML output (only escaped SQL quotes). Else path: completely unsafe. SAST should report: potential XSS on sql and else paths.

hints.message: htmlEscape only helps if outputting to HTML, SQL escaping doesn't prevent XSS.
The else branch has zero sanitisation.
Only html path is safe. sql and else paths are vulnerable to XSS.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Full Codebase Taint Map
practice_exercises.description: Map all taint flows in a multi-class application.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Taint analysis is how tools think like attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Flow 1: req.getParameter -> dao.search(q) -> SQL concatenation -> SQL Injection (CWE-89)
Flow 2: req.getParameter -> model('query') -> th:utext -> XSS (CWE-79) (th:utext doesn't escape!)
2 vulnerabilities from 1 tainted source reaching 2 different sinks

hints.message: The parameter 'q' flows to two different sinks.
th:utext renders unescaped HTML (use th:text for safe output).
2 flows: SQLi via DAO, XSS via th:utext. Both from same getParameter.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: SonarQube Quality Gate Setup
practice_exercises.description: Configure a quality gate that blocks insecure code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Configuring tools correctly is half the battle!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Block: new_vulnerabilities > 0 (any new vuln blocks release)
Block: new_bugs > 0 (any new bug blocks)
Warn: coverage < 80% (code coverage target)
Warn: new_code_smells > 10 (too many code smells)

hints.message: Blockers stop the release. Warnings alert but allow.
Zero new vulnerabilities is the strictest but safest gate.
Block on vulns/bugs, warn on coverage/smells.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: SpotBugs Maven Configuration
practice_exercises.description: Write Maven config to run SpotBugs automatically.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Configuring tools correctly is half the battle!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
artifactId: spotbugs-maven-plugin
effort: Max (most thorough analysis)
threshold: Medium (report medium+ issues)
failOnError: true (break build on bugs)
plugins groupId: com.h3xstream.findsecbugs (security-focused rules)

hints.message: spotbugs-maven-plugin runs SpotBugs during Maven build.
effort:Max for thorough analysis, threshold:Medium for security.
Max effort, Medium threshold, failOnError true, findsecbugs plugin for security rules.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: OWASP ZAP Configuration
practice_exercises.description: Set up OWASP ZAP for a DAST scan.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Configuring tools correctly is half the battle!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Scan: Full (passive first to map the app, then active to attack)
Auth: Configure login script/credentials to test authenticated areas
Threshold: Medium (balance between noise and coverage)
Results: Generate HTML report, fail CI if HIGH alerts found

hints.message: Full scan = passive mapping + active testing.
Authentication is critical — otherwise you only test the login page.
Full scan with auth, medium threshold, fail on HIGH alerts.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: AFL Dictionary File
practice_exercises.description: Create a dictionary file for fuzzing a web server.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Configuring tools correctly is half the battle!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
"GET "
"POST "
"HTTP/1.1"
"Content-Length: "
"Transfer-Encoding: chunked"
"../../../"
"<script>"
"' OR 1=1--"
"%00"
"\r\n\r\n"
"/etc/passwd"
"127.0.0.1"

hints.message: Include HTTP methods, headers, attack payloads.
../ for path traversal, SQL keywords, XSS tags.
Mix valid HTTP tokens with attack payloads for maximum coverage.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Interpret SonarQube Dashboard
practice_exercises.description: Read a SonarQube project dashboard.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Configuring tools correctly is half the battle!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. Failed because of 2 new vulnerabilities (gate blocks on new vulns)
2. The 2 new vulnerabilities (highest security risk)
3. 62% is below industry standard (80%+ recommended)
4. 8.3% of code is copy-pasted (refactoring opportunity)
5. Sum of estimated fix time for all issues

hints.message: New vulnerabilities usually trigger quality gate failures.
Fix vulnerabilities before bugs, bugs before smells.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Write CodeQL Query
